title: How to Earn a Billion Dollars
gist: Paul Graham argues a billion-dollar fortune is mathematically reachable without cheating: it reduces to just two variables — monthly growth rate and how long growth continues — and walks through the compounding math (e.g. 15%/month for 60 months = ~4384x) to show startups routinely hit growth rates that, in a big market, produce billionaires automatically. The path to those growth rates is building something users love enough to tell friends, found by young founders making what they and their friends want rather than consciously hunting for ideas.

insights.claim: Startup-driven wealth is fully determined by just two numbers — the monthly growth rate and how long that growth continues — so becoming a billionaire is a compounding outcome, not evidence of wrongdoing.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Reduces 'how do founders get rich' to two trackable inputs an investor can underwrite: growth rate and market durability.
insights.quote: There are two numbers that determine how big a startup gets, and thus how rich its founders become: the growth rate and how long it continues.
insights.locator: There are two numbers that determine

insights.claim: Neither of the two variables behind billion-dollar outcomes can be faked: growing 15% a month is routinely done without cheating, and the second variable (market size) is something cheating cannot affect, so a billion dollars is provably earnable honestly.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Counters the 'you can't earn a billion without cheating' narrative with a structural argument rather than anecdote.
insights.quote: If it's impossible to make a billion dollars without cheating, which of those two numbers is impossible?
insights.locator: But now you at least understand, from having done the math

insights.claim: A startup with $2M growing at 93% a month needs only ~9.45 months (log base 1.93 of 500) to reach a billion-dollar valuation, so 'a couple million growing fast' and 'a billionaire' are nine and a half months apart, not radically different.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Reframes early traction: a high monthly growth rate is functionally close to a unicorn outcome, which is why growth rate is the first thing to underwrite.
insights.quote: A couple million and 93% growth are not, in fact, radically different from a billion. They're nine and a half months apart.
insights.locator: That is how many months of 93% growth

insights.claim: Revenue growing at a conservative 15% per month compounds to ~4384x over 60 months (5 years), turning $10k/month into ~$44M/month (~$526M/year) and making a typical-ownership founder a billionaire.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Quantifies the bar: 15%/month sustained for five years is the threshold for billion-dollar outcomes, a concrete monitoring target.
insights.quote: If you're currently making ten thousand a month, in five years you'll be making about 44 million a month, or 526 million a year.
insights.locator: If your revenues grow at 15% a month

insights.claim: Real startup growth fronts-loads (faster than 15%/month in year 1, slower by year 4) but lands in roughly the same place, making it possible to be a billionaire by age thirty if you start in your early twenties.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Variable growth paths still converge on the same outcome, so investors should weight the integral of growth over the curve's shape.
insights.locator: In the real world, growth rates tend to slow down

insights.claim: Consistent monthly growth requires building something users like so much they tell their friends, which produces exponential growth via referral; this is why YC asks founders their growth rate first — it reveals whether they built the right thing.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Growth rate is a proxy diagnostic for product-market fit; weak growth signals the wrong product regardless of narrative.
insights.quote: To grow at a consistent rate every month, you have to make something so good that people tell their friends about it.
insights.locator: Especially about that first number, the growth rate

insights.claim: The key to a successful startup is understanding some group of users so deeply that you can build exactly what they want — empathy, not exploitation, is what produces the love that drives referral-led exponential growth.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Identifies the underlying durable input behind growth rate: deep user understanding, which is what YC screens founders for.
insights.quote: if you want to start a successful startup, the key is not exploitation but empathy.
insights.locator: There are other ways to get rich than by starting startups

themes.theme: Young founders should build for themselves

insights.claim: Young founders should make what they and their friends want because their own needs are an unusually valuable signal — what young people start using now predicts what everyone uses in ten years — while their guesses about other people's needs are a poor signal.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Provides a heuristic for spotting future demand early: track what young early-adopters are already using.
insights.quote: Whatever you and your friends start using now, everyone is going to be using in ten years.
insights.locator: You're young, and usually young founders should make something

insights.claim: An idea only needs to appeal to you and your friends, not have wide initial appeal, because predicting future demand means a niche beachhead (e.g. DNA tools, drones) can expand into adjacent markets as the market grows.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Small initial TAM is not disqualifying if the founders' niche predicts a future mass market; underwrite trajectory, not current size.
insights.quote: All you need is a beachhead in the territory of unsatisfied need that you can expand from.
insights.locator: Don't worry about the second number, the market size

themes.theme: Best ideas come from not looking

insights.claim: The best startup ideas are found not by consciously searching for them — which makes founders too conservative and lops off the outliers — but by working on projects with friends that weren't even meant to be companies, the way Apple, Google, and Facebook started.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Signals where breakout ideas originate: side projects and 'cool' builds, not deliberate idea hunts — relevant for sourcing early-stage deals.
insights.quote: the way to get the very best startup ideas is not to look for startup ideas.
insights.locator: How do you get an idea like that?

insights.claim: The very best startup ideas tend to sound lame or preposterous at first — Apple, Facebook, and Airbnb all seemed like bad ideas, and YC funded Airbnb despite thinking the idea was bad, only because they liked the founders.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Pattern for investors: a counterintuitive, easily-dismissed idea paired with strong founders can be a feature, not a red flag.
insights.quote: We funded Airbnb, and we thought the idea was bad. The reason we funded them was just that we liked the founders.
insights.locator: Imagine what a bad idea Apple or Facebook or Airbnb seemed

insights.claim: Building random things that seem cool to you is far from random because you predict future demand, so your unconscious sense of what's cool reliably points at good startup ideas — as with Justin.TV, a man live-streaming his life from a head camera, which became Twitch.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Apparently absurd early products can pivot into large outcomes; dismissing on first impression risks missing the next Twitch.
insights.quote: Whatever you build couldn't possibly sound more preposterous than a startup we funded in 2006 called Justin.TV.
insights.locator: This is one of those cases where your unconscious mind knows more

insights.claim: Politicians distrust startup wealth and assume cheating because they don't understand the math of exponential growth, so seemingly impossible riches read to them as fraud rather than compounding.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: Explains a recurring policy/regulatory bias against fast-growing wealth; relevant to anticipating wealth-tax and anti-billionaire political risk.
insights.quote: They don't understand the math of exponential growth, so when they see people becoming what seems to them impossibly rich, they assume they must have cheated.

insights.claim: Beliefs about how people get rich should be formed by observing how it is actually done in the present world, not from ideology, movies, or centuries-old historical examples.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham Essays
insights.so_what: An epistemic caution: mental models of wealth creation drawn from outdated sources misprice the modern startup path.
insights.quote: You can't let your beliefs about this be determined by ideology, or movies, or historical examples that are centuries old.
insights.locator: How people become rich in your society
